claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is the child? Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me about each of them and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my three children actually. 

I've got Alex who's 5, Blake who's 21, and Charlie who's 13.

Can you tell me if I can claim for any of them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! I'll assess eligibility for each of your three children: Alex (5), Blake (21), and Charlie (13).

Let me work through each child based on their age:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me navigate properly for Blake by going back to the age check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I have the information for Blake. Let me continue assessing Alex and Charlie (both under 16). They've reached the same point in the decision tree, so I need to check if they're working less than 24 hours per week:

**For Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 13):**

Since they are both under 16, they automatically qualify based on age. However, I need to confirm a few more things:

1. **Are Alex and Charlie working less than 24 hours per week?** (Given their ages of 5 and 13, I'd expect they're either not working or working very limited hours, if at all - but please confirm)

---

**For Blake (age 21):**

I can already tell you that unfortunately, **Blake is not eligible** for Child Benefit. The rules state that a child must be either:
- Under 16 years old, OR
- Aged 16-19 and in approved education or training

At 21, Blake no longer qualifies as a "child" for Child Benefit purposes, regardless of their circumstances.

---

So let's continue with Alex and Charlie - can you confirm they are not working 24+ hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, that's a shame about Blake, but I understand.

As for Alex and Charlie - no, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 5, so obviously not working, and Charlie is 13 and just goes to school. They don't have any jobs or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense - thank you for confirming!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, this means they are not personally claiming benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

Given their ages (5 and 13), this would be unusual, but I need to confirm - are they both free from claiming these benefits themselves?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all that sort of thing for the family. They don't claim anything themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's exactly what I'd expect - thank you for confirming!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great progress! Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie in any kind of apprenticeship?**

Given their ages (5 and 13), I would assume not - apprenticeships typically start at age 16 or older. But just to confirm:

- Is Alex (5) in an apprenticeship? 
- Is Charlie (13) in an apprenticeship?

(I expect the answer is no for both, but I need to check this off!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. Alex is only 5 - far too young for that! And Charlie is 13, still in regular school. Neither of them are in any apprenticeships.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I thought - just needed to confirm!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about **your** residency status (as the person claiming the benefit).

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (the most common situation)
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** AND are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have **pre-settled status** AND are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** AND have sufficient resources to support yourself (and aren't claiming certain income-based benefits)
6. You have **pre-settled status** AND are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Which of these applies to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester, so that's straightforward - I normally live here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's nice and straightforward!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Charlie.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

You qualify as responsible if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with the child** (the most common situation)
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Charlie, or are you responsible for them in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Charlie live at home with me. They're with me full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect - that's the most straightforward situation!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Next question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This could be:
- **No other claimant** - no one else is claiming for them
- **Other claimant has agreed to transfer** - someone else was claiming but has agreed you should claim instead
- **Disputed situation** - there's a disagreement about who should claim

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me - I'm their parent and they live with me, so nobody else is getting it for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That keeps things simple - thank you!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! I need to check about the children's current living situation:

**Where are Alex and Charlie currently living?**

- **Normal living** - at home with you (the most common situation)
- **In hospital** - currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - in local authority care
- **Fostered** - in a fostering arrangement

I expect both are living at home with you normally, but just need to confirm!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex lives at home with me normally - no issues there.

But Charlie has been in care for a couple of months. He's with the local authority, but he does come home to stay with me on the weekends, so he's not there full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying - that's an important distinction. Let me check the rules for each child's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is helpful! Let me ask you a bit more about Charlie's situation:

**For Charlie (in local authority care):**

You mentioned he comes home on weekends. The rules say Child Benefit can continue if:
- The child has been in care for **8 weeks or less**, OR
- The child spends **at least 24 hours per week at home**

You said Charlie has been in care for "a couple of months" (so likely more than 8 weeks). 

**Does Charlie spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?** 

(For example, if he comes home Friday evening and stays until Sunday afternoon, that would likely be 24+ hours)

---

**For Alex (living at home normally):**

Just one final question - is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance? (This would be unusual for a child living at home with their parent, but I need to check.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Charlie - yes, he spends at least 24 hours a week with me. He comes home on the weekends, so that's definitely more than 24 hours each week.

And for Alex - no, the council isn't paying anything for him. He just lives at home with me and I take care of everything for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent - that's great news for both children!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Alex is eligible! Now I just have one final question about Charlie:

**For Charlie (in care but home 24+ hours per week):**

Since Charlie is in local authority care, is the **local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance** while he's in their care?

(This is about whether the council is financially supporting Charlie's care placement, not about any benefits you might receive personally.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local authority does pay for Charlie while he's in care with them. They provide the maintenance for him at the care placement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. I now have all the information I need to give you the final outcome for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

I've now completed the assessment for all three of your children. Here are the results:

---

### ✅ Alex (age 5) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the eligibility criteria:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in his own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- You live with him and are responsible for him
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council isn't paying for his care

---

### ❌ Blake (age 21) - **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, Blake is too old to qualify. Child Benefit is only available for children who are:
- Under 16, or
- Aged 16-19 and in approved education or training

At 21, Blake no longer qualifies regardless of their circumstances.

---

### ❌ Charlie (age 13) - **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, although Charlie is under 16 and spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you, you cannot claim Child Benefit for him because the **local authority is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance** at his care placement.

If Charlie's situation changes in the future (for example, if he returns home full-time and the council is no longer paying for his care), you may become eligible to claim for him at that point.

---

## Next Steps

To claim Child Benefit for **Alex**, you can:
- Apply online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)
- Or call the Child Benefit helpline on 0300 200 3100

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about these results?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. He meets all the criteria: (1) He is under 16 years old, (2) He is not working, (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right, (4) He is not in an apprenticeship, (5) The claimant (parent) normally lives in the UK, (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, (8) He lives at home normally, and (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 21) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. At 21 years old, Blake exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit. To qualify, a child must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. As Blake is 20 or over, they no longer qualify as a 'child' for Child Benefit purposes, regardless of any other circumstances.

Charlie (age 13) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Although Charlie meets most criteria (under 16, not working, no benefits in own right, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK and is responsible for him, no other claimant), he is currently in local authority care. While Charlie does spend at least 24 hours per week at home (which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue), the local council is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance at the care placement. When the council is financially supporting the child's care, Child Benefit cannot be claimed.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake (age 21) is too old to qualify - Child Benefit stops when a child turns 20, even if in education. Charlie (age 13), despite being under 16 and spending weekends at home, is ineligible because the local authority is paying for his care placement. The claimant can claim Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

As Blake is 20 or over, they no longer qualify as a 'child' for Child Benefit purposes, regardless of any other circumstances.

Charlie (age 13) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Although Charlie meets most criteria (under 16, not working, no benefits in own right, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK and is responsible for him, no other claimant), he is currently in local authority care. While Charlie does spend at least 24 hours per week at home (which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue), the local council is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance at the care placement. When the council is financially supporting the child's care, Child Benefit cannot be claimed.
